data.title: Meta: Growth Solid, Innovation Costs Mounting
data.description:
My Hold view on Meta Platforms (META) in May 2025 has worked out somehow. It is now down by ~10%, although the S&P 500 is still up by ~12% from the time of my last analysis. Importantly, Meta was up by 20% at some point in August/September, stretching my thesis, before a recent sharp pullback following Q3 results and management commentary.

Despite the pullback now, I feel Meta is not ready to be upgraded to a Buy. The core issue I face in buying Meta now is a lack of conviction in its capital discipline and a defined moat positioning for its futuristic investments. The core business is doing reasonably well, but margins are getting stretched with no clear path for return on investments.

Core Business Reasonably Strong

First up, revenues. Revenues reported in Q3 were actually up by a staggering 26%, and aided by the right channels. Active users were up by 8% YoY. Ad impressions grew by 14%, and the average price per ad also showed double-digit growth. All very good so far. The core business seems to tick the right boxes and is doing reasonably well.

In terms of guidance, the midpoint of $57.5b is still a healthy ~19% YoY uptick in topline. However, seen in the perspective of recent growth levels in the mid-twenties, this seems to be a slight detour from the acceleration in growth seen in the past quarters. In fact, Q4 has been a seasonally bullish month. While that makes for a stiffer comp, it is equally true that the revenue uptick seen in Q4 in the past seems relatively subdued this time around.

There are some other drags in headline numbers too. One time tax adjustments meant a blow for the GAAP EPS. However, normalized numbers beat expectations, so that is not a cause for concern. What impacts the outlook, though, is a $2b increase in expected expenses for the full year from the earlier guided $117b midpoint. Management also highlighted growing regulatory drags in Europe (less personalized ads offered) and youth-related trials in the US in 2026. However, the greater cause for concern on fresh Meta positions lies beyond the revenue guidance and cost increases.

Cost of Building a Moat

At the core of my hesitancy around Meta in May were capital allocation and ROI concerns around a continued investment in Reality Labs and capex as a whole. That story remains unchanged broadly. In fact, market reactions after Q3 results suggest a correction around the same concerns that had me thinking in May.

Reality Labs continues to drag income by $4.4b almost consistently now - a very capital-intensive experimental bet that becomes difficult to value. The inflection to a viable commercial offering is still looking distant. Despite an encouraging ~74% YoY growth in revenues here, the company effectively still burns ~$9.5 for every dollar of revenue generated. The Q4 guidance is not encouraging either. Although the lower Reality Labs revenue in Q4 is more an operational issue (due to the absence of a new VR headset launch in 2025) than a true demand caution, it still could stretch the annual losses in Reality Labs alone to $17-18b. That is meaningfully worse than even what I anticipated in May.

I find the Reality Lab bet more unclear strategically looking at the absence of a 2025 release of the Quest headset. It is unclear how Meta intends to grow this segment, positioning it between spatial computing or the metaverse theme, or either. Ambiguous signals like switching the terminology from metaverse to spatial computing to AI glasses only reinforce my sense that Meta is still probing for a narrative that resonates. Competitors have already captured clearer mindshare. Apple seems to dominate the spatial computing story with Vision Pro, and OpenAI has seized the AI-assistant narrative. The lack of clarity, as far as I am concerned, is disconcerting because losses are piling up at a time when clearer profitability is the need of the hour. Even if Meta generates 20% operating margins from Reality Lab's $100b investments (projected at the current rate of $15b per year) by 2028, it would still need 5-10 years to recover the cumulative costs.

What Reality Labs does to investors like me, is make overall R&D and capital allocation look riskier than they should be. The AI race is still in its initial stages. Investors do flock to bet on capex intensive capability building in Google and Palantir, with the same amount of uncertainties, where Meta's history makes capex increase announcements a cause for caution.

Meta has announced an increased capex spending of $70-72b for the year, not significantly up from the earlier estimates of the $66-72b midpoint. However, Meta has emphasized a continued capex uptick in 2026 to address its growing compute needs. What makes the increased capex spending concerning is a lack of a transparent monetization path. Llama 4 integration across apps is solid progress. Even custom silicon investments (the Rivos acquisition) de-risk Meta from Nvidia dependencies. The AI narrative still seems to be centered around ad and content recommendation improvements, which are already embedded in existing FoA revenues.

Meta's investments in data centers have execution and delay risks. If cloud providers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure) successfully monetize AI compute through customer access, Meta's captive infrastructure becomes less of a moat. There is no indicative data being discussed yet around utilization rates, inference costs per query, and timeline expectations to measure the potential impact on monetization.

Margin Compression Concerns

The phase of "difficult to measure" investments over operational discipline is already hurting the operating margins, which have receded from ~48% in Q4 2025 to just over 40% now. Structural decay in operating margins due to investments is not something hurting Google or Microsoft because they have lower proportional exposures in moonshots because of their size and structure. I was already wary of margin compression, and there has not been any respite on margins since May. Even the ROIC trajectory has turned south, further fueling the monetization concerns.

Valuations Rightly Pulling Back

The reason why I am still holding Meta instead of selling (given my uncertainty around its investment monetization path) is because its valuations are relatively conducive compared to a Google or a Microsoft, despite the high capex burn. I have used a trailing EV to FCF metric to show the relative valuation comfort in Meta, because it takes into account the heavy capex and losses from Reality Labs. Forward estimates will be less reliable given the uncertain monetization path and timelines. Despite that drag, Meta is trading at ~34x EV to trailing FCF, reasonably lower than Microsoft's ~48x and Google's ~46x mark. The recent corrections post Q3 make this valuation gap more pronounced, making shorts difficult.

At the same time, this valuation opportunity does not imply a buy scenario either. The discount is justified. Meta's lower multiple reflects Reality Labs' losses, AI monetization ambiguity, and margin compression. Critically, forward EV/FCF could be far worse if FCF continues to be suppressed due to capex surges with no clear monetization uplift.​

Not Catching This Falling Knife

The close to 25% corrections from the top are compelling, but Meta's thesis does not provide measurable levers to base a buy thesis on yet. Qualitatively, its moat in the AI race is unclear, and the track record of a Reality Labs bleed is not helping.

If I look beyond fundamentals towards momentum or narrative plays too, this fall is not a great time to invest. Meta has a history of steep corrections. Between the peaks and troughs of 2021-2022, Meta corrected by 75%. Even in the April sell-off earlier this year, a fall of ~33% was seen. Meta is not a knife that should be caught when falling. Even for momentum trades, a 50% retracement from the lows makes for a better trading ploy historically (looking at the last 5 years data), for those concerned about missing out on the cheaper prices today or being able to time a bottom.

As a long-term fundamental investor, I will wait for AI monetization to show up as incremental revenue by mid-2026 or a clear path to be outlined and visible. I would also like Meta to show lower losses in its Reality Labs play (increasing trend currently), and that is both because of its numerical impact and because of getting more confidence around Meta's capital discipline overall. I will also watch for some stability in operating margins before jumping in with fresh capital. Till then, Meta is a hold.

data.title: Selectivity Drives Outperformance In Europe
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

Open-ended core fund returns gain momentum

The European ODCE index posted net cash returns of +0.83% (local currency), marking a sixth straight positive quarter and accelerating from +0.24% in 2Q. Capital returns turned positive at +0.08%, the third positive performance in the past four quarters. Still, performance remains below the +1.51% posted in 1Q25 and +0.94% in 4Q24.

Across the Atlantic, the U.S. ODCE index returned +0.52% in 3Q25, with income returns of +0.79% offset by negative capital returns of -0.26%. Historically, the U.S. ODCE index has outperformed in 71% of quarters since 3Q11, with annualized returns of +6.4% versus +3.3% in Europe. Yet, since early 2023, performance has reversed, with Europe outperforming in eight out of eleven quarters.

Quarterly total returns of European vs. U.S. real estate funds

3Q 2011–3Q 2025

The recent outperformance of Europe is consistent with our view that the continent may benefit from improving economic conditions, wider cap rates, and lower sovereign debt yields making leverage more accretive. Indeed, as of 2Q25, Europe’s unlevered asset level total returns across stood +6.9% over the trailing four quarters compared to +4.7% for the U.S. over the same period. These results are consistent with our midyear forecast of 6–7% total returns for Europe in 2025, versus roughly ±5% in the U.S.

Another layer to the story

Macro tailwinds alone don’t explain the divergence. Return dispersion across funds has also widened. Over the past year, the median net return for European ODCE funds was +4.2%, with top-quartile managers at +5.3% and bottom-quartile managers at +2.5%.

U.S. ODCE dispersion is even starker: top-quartile funds delivered 1-year annualized gross total returns of +5.7% vs. 4.9% for the second quartile, 4% for the third quartile, and a notably weak 0.3% for the bottom quartile. In other words, the top U.S. funds are outperforming, but laggards are far underperforming their European peers.

Focused strategies continue to outperform diversified

The broader INREV All-Fund Index delivered a stronger +1.05% total return, supported by income (+0.67%) and capital growth (+0.38%). This is consistent with historical trends, as the All-Fund Index has outperformed the European ODCE index in nearly 74% of quarters since 3Q11, driven in part by stronger single-sector performance.

Single-sector funds continued to outpace multi-sector peers (+0.55% total return), with the notable exception of office. Sector-level returns included:

  • Residential: +1.87% (+1.15% capital growth)
  • Industrial: +1.41% (+0.80% income growth)
  • Retail: +0.96% (+0.66% income growth)
  • Office: +0.27% (0.50% income growth)

Comparison of European fund returns

3Q25 vs. trailing 4-quarter total

Bottom line

European real estate markets continue to demonstrate steady momentum, with consistent performance supported by improving macro conditions and tighter return dispersion. While U.S. funds still lead at the top end of the return spectrum, European strategies are benefiting from greater stability across managers and sectors. In the current environment, where macro tailwinds and manager skill both matter, selectivity both in geography and strategy remains the key to capturing outperformance.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tenaris Q3 And 2025E Update: Continued Risk, Especially With Market Downside
data.description:
I have been covering Tenaris (TS) for a few years at this point. It is one of those businesses where I have been able to do a good entry and a good rotation of my position back before April of 2025. The company then significantly declined and has been under pressure for some time. If you followed my targets, then you probably reinvested or at least considered as much in the last 6 months.

The recent report and market trends saw the company bounce back up to the level where I previously sold. This makes the company a good RoR for those who bought cheap before, but it does not make it, to my mind, a good investment for new investors unless fundamentals and upside have changed.

And that is what we are going to look at here.

Tenaris has, in my view, a mix of valuation and results problems (meaning recent results). The company has cut its forecasts, and analysts have cut their forecasts for the company as well. The volatility in the market is something to be expected given the company's exposure.

Generally, I have found straight oil and energy companies to be better investments than something like this - both in terms of yield and potential upside. Tenaris and companies like it serving the oil industry are investable at the right yield and upside. The recent 3Q25 came out at the end of October, about 3 weeks ago from the time of writing, and it is these results we will look at to determine what price you should pay for an energy service company in a market that seems set to go negative (judging by recent trends and this week's ups and downs) in the near or medium term.

Tenaris has a lot going for it. I like its dividend, its Luxembourg domicile, and its APAC and African exposure. It trades and operates in an overall tricky market, wedged between low-cost producers and operators/customers demanding cheaper products, while being inherently a premium product company (in a segment increasingly moving towards commoditization).

This requires careful consideration.

Tenaris - Upside is there, but at this price, it is a tricky one

Positives first, including positives for 3Q25 and for the coming full-year results. This is not a company in any immediate danger. The fact that it has a net cash position of $3.5B at the end of 3Q25 should count for something - even if overall 3Q25 results were not necessarily all that great.

The company's business idea is a solid and simple one. It operates in a segment where its competition consists of companies in straight steel industry, like ArcelorMittal (MT), which also cover, but also smaller ones like Aperam. In straight oil service, we can look at Halliburton and SLB (SLB), also Baker Hughes (BKR). As we will see in the valuation portion of the article, one of the problems now facing Tenaris is that peer valuation levels are simply a lot more attractive here than they once were, and relative to Tenaris, could be an argument for investing before Tenaris itself.

3Q25 was okay but not great. Net sales decreased (but overall were decent, especially the increase in welding), but operating income was up somewhat, and FCF decreased to less than $135M due to WC increase and high inventory levels. Tenaris is one of the more significant Tariff-impacted companies that I cover - and the full flow of tariff impacts has not yet come to the company either.

So there are headwinds left, which leads me to believe that this recent surge is going to see a downturn again.

Primary global energy demand sees no decline or weakness that would make Tenaris a bad company to invest in at the right price. Despite renewable investments, the level of CapEx in E&P remains elevated and is likely to remain elevated. This is one of the primary indicators I track for how these companies do, and for the full year, we saw an increase, with a slowed growth rate from 2023.

I expect either a slight increase or flat trends in 2025E as well, with one exception being Brazil and Petrobras, where I believe the CapEx will be increasing further.

The risk profile for Tenaris remains as before - high-end product sales will never return to 2014 levels, when the entire market for those products turned down (the deep-water offshore customer/order decline), and combined with one of the closest peers pushing into low-cost production, we are likely to see further pricing pressure. How I consider this is that margins are unlikely to grow from here - the company will have to grow by volume, and recent trends tell us that this seems somewhat unlikely. Especially since the US shale segment has moved from Premium to semipremium products, which is (combined with tariffs) what is causing pain here. Shale was, and arguably still is, Tenaris' most important end market. Recent results also highlight slowing drill activity in key markets, importantly, both Canada and the US.

Volatility in the macro is coming from China's continued level of increased export of steel. The EU is applying protectionist measures, as is the US.

One of the more important potential upsides in 3Q25 was the election in Argentina, which implies that there is now a place for foreign capital for development projects, which would require products from companies like Tenaris. This pro-investment climate is likely to trickle down to this company as well, in assets like Vaca Muerta, with an expected number of rigs operating in Argentina.

Whenever the rig count goes up, and Tenaris is there, you can expect upside. Further upside potential is from further share buybacks. Remember, Tenaris has a controlling family, and this changes these considerations somewhat. Tenaris still has $600m out of the original $1.2B worth of buybacks in its announcement, and the company has confirmed its intention to fully execute that second tranche of buybacks. This will likely also support the share price and valuation growth.

My forecast for Tenaris is slow growth or flat development. In 2025E, I expect a slight decline from 2024, as the overall growth level for CapEx moves down, and coupled with increased competition and tariffs, I do not believe a return to the $12.5B+ level of sales revenue until 2028E at the earliest. Same with EBIT/EBITDA - I expect it to be flat from today's levels, about $2.8B annualized in the next 3-4 years. The upside for investors will come from internal improvements and buybacks, which require (in my view) investors to buy shares at a lower valuation to ensure that underperformance is an unlikely outcome. Current GAAP forecasts confirm this and are in line with my own.

The company's advantages, including Rig Direct and the new Bay Mills asset, will work for the company and increase earnings and lower tariff impacts, with US-based production - but I view this as overall unlikely to fully offset the negative impact of increased competition, flattening out CapEx potential, and more stable than significantly growing demand.

That makes this, at heart, a pure valuation play and little else.

The valuation for the company is currently as follows.

Valuation for Tenaris - Upside possible, but better at lower multiples.

The company lacks a moat, despite its Rig Direct program. Its assets related to premium production in the OCTG sector are a competitive advantage, not a moat in my assessment. Differentiation here is minimal; aside from that, premiumization. The volatility in the end markets can be expressed with the example of the Vallourec/Tenaris decline back in the shale boom of -07 to -14, and the result is a premiumized OCTG market that is down to less than one-third of demand.

Regardless of how you view or "like" the company, this is something to keep in mind.

Tenaris' appeal depends on how you view its valuation. As you know from my work, I tend toward conservatism when it comes to applying multiples. So when the company in question has a 5-year multiple of X, I tend to listen (especially if that aligns with the longer-term multiples). For Tenaris, that multiple is 9.9x. The company currently trades at 11x. Based on the likelihood for earnings decline or flat development (see above), and the current forecast of less than 0.2% AEPS growth on average for the next 3 years (which, based on trends, I agree with), I cannot see a good argument for applying any higher multiple than 12-13 on this company - and even that is stretching it.

We are talking about valuing a company that reports little to no growth while in an inflated market where it competes with premium products in an industry that has shown a liking for sub-premium. The volatility in forecast accuracy can be confirmed by looking at trends, with less than 10% accuracy over the past 10 years, with about a 50/50 chance to either beat or miss those targets. Not the best track record, nor the best set of expectations for a good performance here.

It is this sort of trend that ensures that even at a relatively high 12x P/E forecast, the company does not even generate 10% annualized RoR, even with its 4%+ yield included.

Based on this, the company would either need to grow earnings significantly more than expected, which, based on the macro and sector backdrop, I consider to be unlikely, or trade at a higher premium, which, again, based on company growth, I consider to be very unlikely. Beyond that, I do not really see much chance of this company generating better returns.

Then we include the context of the per valuation. SLB (SLB) is a company I have not extensively covered beyond peripheral mentions in other articles, but it trades more attractively relative to its historicals, has a 3%+ yield, and has an A credit rating and lower debt than Tenaris. It has an upside to a 13-14x P/E of 18% annualized, and for this company, that is a discount. Halliburton (HAL) has a 15%+ annualized upside to a P/E of below 13x, with a BBB+ credit, and a lower yield. Also, more attractive than Tenaris here.

Based on the above-presented outlooks and estimates, I consider Tenaris to be a "HOLD". On more conservative growth estimates, I shift my PT back down to below €16/share, with a fair value of €20/share for a 15% annualized upside to a conservative estimate. For the ADR TS, this comes to $37/share, given that this is a 2x ADR.

Risks & Considerations

The negatives for the company, I believe, will ensure that we will never see the valuations that are causing the lofty targets we see here. The sales of high-end products were at truly massive levels before 2014 – it is unlikely this will ever happen again. These are the primary risks.

Rivals are moving to low-cost commoditized production of products, which will be interesting for producers in areas like Brazil and China, and there has been an entry into Tenaris segments with local US OCTG producers as well, which represents the secondary risk to the company. Beyond this, there are macro and other factors over which the company lacks controlling influence, but that are nonetheless worth considering.

That means my thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

Tenaris is a leading company in the attractive segment of energy services. The company has a leading position in several markets and has one of the better balance sheets in the segment and industry, with extremely low leverage and high overall interest coverage.

Tenaris may not have the highest yield or even a high yield for this sort of company, but it has a history of outperforming the market over time. Investors who bought 20 years ago have done very well for themselves with over 12% annualized return, inclusive of dividends - this is despite the downcycle lows we saw in 2016, and that makes the company a potentially attractive opportunity here.

I consider the company to be a "HOLD" below a share price of €16/share, lowered from my last article, making it unattractive here. This translates to an ADR PT for ticker TS of $37/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of €16/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2025. The company fulfills an insufficient number of my fundamental criteria for investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brightstar Lottery: Forecasting Three Years Of Cash, Dividends, And Buybacks
data.description:
A fat special dividend in July, a mountain of buybacks, and a roadmap stretching years ahead—that’s what you’d be sitting on if you’d followed my ‘Buy’ call back in March.

Two out of my three pieces since then have even beaten the S&P.

Not bad at all!

We’ve tracked everything from the IGT handoff to Brightstar Lottery (BRSL) to the consortium locking in the LottoItalia renewal, even though a lot of people expected the license to stay with Flutter (FLTR)/Sisal.

And here we are again, heading into another quarter.

To be honest, the market didn’t take the Q3 FY 2025 results all that well. The stock’s down about 9% since they came out on November 4th with mixed results out of the hat.

At this point, the stock is indicating an implied upside of ~23% to the Wall St. consensus target and at least 42% to mine at $22. But like I've been saying, the upside (tempting as it looks) isn't the whole picture.

This quarter, they bumped the dividend by 10% ($0.22 per share) and came right out and said they’ll hand back $1.7 billion to shareholders out of the $4 billion they pulled in from the sale by FY 2028.

We’re dealing with a straight-up cash cow here.

I'll sketch out the model right after we run through the quarter, just so you get a real feel for how much shareholder yield should be on the table going forward.

Jackpots Are Back

Brightstar's revenue had been taking a hit the last two quarters thanks to softer comparable sales at the big jackpot spots in the U.S., but that hangover didn't show up this time around.

It's worth keeping in mind we're up against very tough comps.

FY 2024 was packed with jackpots, and that pushed betting sharply higher (Q2 alone saw comparable sales climb about 22.5%). And, since Brightstar earns a percentage of recurring revenue from these bets (in addition to LMAs, of course), it ends up benefiting from these accumulation periods.

This quarter, Powerball topped out at a $1.8 billion jackpot, making it the second-biggest in U.S. lottery history.

So, you can imagine that many rushed to place their bets.

For Brightstar, that translated into a 69.2% jump in comparable sales year over year, enough to wipe out the double-digit declines from the first half, although comps are still down 10.5% year to date. Instant sales were up 1.6% year over year, a bit faster than what we saw in the previous quarter.

Overall, comparable sales in the United States remained virtually flat year-over-year.

Looking at Italy and the rest of the world (places like the Czech Republic, Barbados, and the Caribbean lotteries) comparable sales kept climbing at a solid pace. In Italy, tweaks like the new Miliardario and the push toward pricier €25 tickets helped speed things up even more.

You can see the bigger picture here:

Total revenue came in at $629 million, up 7.3% from a year ago. For Brightstar, this was the strongest quarterly revenue growth in at least two years. And the funny thing? Even with that kind of number, they still came in at least $4.8 million short of what Wall St. was looking for.

Maybe folks just got carried away with the jackpot story? Could it be? But to me, this was still a strong lift in revenue.

Here’s how each piece fed into the quarter’s revenue:

Despite gross margin slipping a bit from last year because of higher POP printing system rollout costs, Brightstar still managed to lift its bottom line thanks to lighter SG&A and lower interest expenses.

Adjusted EBITDA, for example, went from $264 million to $294 million (an 11.3% jump), and net income rose 18.6% to $117 million.

Below I pulled together the recent margins so we can look at them side by side:

Italian Lotto and Temporary Pressure on Cash Flow

Cash flow is where folks usually get confused, especially if Brightstar’s model isn’t something you look at every day.

Since a large part of their revenue comes from long-term lottery contracts, they have to pay those license fees upfront. Because that counts as an operating expense, it drags OCF into the red even when the business is doing just fine.

Just look at this quarter, and you will understand.

OCF came in at –$446 million, mainly because they paid $579 million to the Italian Customs and Monopolies Agency—just a slice of the €2.23 billion they still owe under the license.

But does this mean they are terrible cash generators? Not at all.

Adjusted for that payment, Brightstar would’ve put up about $573 million in OCF and as much as $334 million in FCF.

It's still very much a cash cow.

All of this became possible thanks to the roughly $4 billion they pulled in from the widely discussed sale of IGT Digital Gaming.

To make it easier for you to visualize what they've done with that money so far, here it is:

Going forward, there are two more payments on the way: about €300 million (likely hitting in Q4 FY 2025) and another €1.4 billion in the first half of FY 2026. Both will keep putting temporary pressure on Brightstar’s cash flow.

Probably, to be able to pay this, they will stretch net leverage to up to 3x EBITDA, as in the long-term target. As they have $2.7 billion in net debt on the balance sheet and they are targeting an adjusted EBITDA of $1.1 billion, we could expect it to end up at $3.3 billion (adding at least another $600 million by the first half of next year).

Great information, good to consider in any model we do going forward, isn't it?

But you might be wondering if running the Italian Lottery is really worth that kind of money. And I’ll tell you straight up—it is.

In the past nine years, the Italian Lotto has had roughly €8 billion in annual sales, which is a massive amount by any standard. Keep in mind they get 6% on wagers plus another 8% on iLottery bets, so you can figure the consortium is raking in at least €480 million a year.

Dividends: Are They Still Protected?

We received a ton of information in the last earnings call (a ton, really!), and one piece of it was that dividends would increase by 10% to $0.22 per share quarterly.

That's $0.88 per share annually, a pretty hefty yield of 5.6%.

Doing some simple math, if we assume they normalize buybacks to low single digits every year from now on, Brightstar would be distributing ~$165.5 million annually in dividends.

Is that sustainable? Well, let's see.

Since they are expecting EBITDA to be $1.1 billion for FY 2025 and to grow steadily at a CAGR of 3% until FY 2028, I'm targeting $1.13 billion for FY 2026. Knowing they're aiming for an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of about 70%, OCF should land somewhere around $800 million in FY 2026. Another piece of information is that Capex will likely remain at $400 million.

We know that the largest chunk of the Italian Lottery payout (~$1.6 billion already converted) will be collected in FY 2026 and will reduce OCF to -$800 million (-$1.2 billion FCF after deducting Capex).

I figure that by FY 2026 they'll probably put part of that cash pile to work (the nearly $1.6 billion they've got sitting around) and add a bit of debt on top. But nothing that would push leverage past their 3x EBITDA target.

They'd be able to claw back those $165.5 million. It wouldn't be painless, but they could get there.

In a normal year like FY 2027, they could probably even increase it given the already normalized FCF of $412 million, covering it by almost 2.5x.

There’s one more move they’ve laid out: $1.7 billion back to shareholders between FY 2025 and FY 2028. So far, they’ve handed out about $900 million, leaving around $800 million still to go.

After the fast-paced buyback last quarter (nearly 6% fewer shares than a year ago), they’ve got another $250 million left under their biannual buyback plan.

Adding it all up, if dividends remain stable over these three years and the entire authorization is carried out, we'd be talking about a total return of $746.5 million, $53.5 million less than they stated.

This smells like more dividend increases on the way. Probably something in the mid-single digits starting in FY 2027, when FCF is no longer under so much pressure.

How Much Can You Expect in Returns?

As I told you before, I continue to expect dividends to deliver mid-single-digit returns annually until FY 2028 (perhaps mid-to-high towards the end of this period).

For share buybacks, I believe they will start flowing more strongly in FY 2027.

So you're basically looking at a mid-single-digit shareholder yield in FY 2026, and then high single-digit returns in FY 2027 and FY 2028. Given what they've laid out so far, that feels like a pretty realistic path.

Bottom Line

In addition to those hefty returns coming from a low-risk model built on recurring revenue and long-term contracts, I also found it interesting that Brightstar pointed out how lottery spending tends to hold up even during recessions.

This is something I was aware of, but I didn't have any graphs of my own.

Note that in the last three major recessions (dotcom bust, the GFC, and even Covid), lottery revenues didn’t crack. They held steady or went up in U.S. From 2020 to 2021, they even increased enough to push the CAGR to 6.7% by 2022.

But it’s not just the returns or the resilience that catch my eye. The multiples are low right now too.

If we take the projected adjusted EBITDA of $1.1 billion for FY 2025 (perhaps slightly less when we unadjust it), we would conclude that the stock is trading at a multiple of ~5x to 6x EBITDA, well below the 5-year average of 7.3x.

You could argue, quite rightly, that after the sale of the Digital division, the market re-rated the stock to adjust it to a slower growth scenario.

But I don't think Brightstar deserved such a low multiple. After all, it's the world's largest pure-play lottery company.

Peers like The Lottery Corporation (OTCPK:LTRCF), ZEAL, and Intralot (OTCPK:IRLTF) are trading at much higher multiples. Pollard Banknote (PBL:CA), which also runs charity games on top of lotteries, is closer to Brightstar, though its 5-year average sits around 9x EBITDA.

In my opinion, 7x EBITDA is fair for Brightstar while it still has its FCF under pressure.

This would give us a target very close to the $22 we were aiming for before.

Similarly, if we model a DCF with that FCF path I mentioned earlier ($1.2 billion in FY 2026, stabilizing at $400 million until FY 2029, when it increases to ~$500 million), we arrive at a very similar result.

And keep in mind that I'm considering a low perpetual growth rate (1.5% annually) and 8.5% to discount it to present value.

That being said, with an upside of ~42%, I'll reiterate my 'Strong Buy' rating here and expect those hefty returns I mentioned.

data.title: The Quality Quandary In European Equities
data.description:
By Thorsten Winkelmann & Marcus Morris-Eyton

How can investors in high-quality companies overcome this year’s performance headwinds?

It’s been a tough year for high-quality stocks in Europe. Yet, despite vexing market conditions, the underlying business features that define quality stocks often remain intact. For investors, there are compelling reasons to maintain conviction in companies with robust profitability and resilient business models—even when short-term returns disappoint.

Recent European market trends have challenged investors who focus on quality. Over the past year, European value stocks outperformed growth and quality stocks by a wide margin (Display), in contrast to the previous decade when all three styles delivered similar returns.

Quality Growth Stocks Have Underperformed in Europe

What’s driven the value rally? First, European financials—the largest sector in the value benchmark—were buoyed by a steep yield curve, which boosted the profitability of lending activity, as regional banks also benefited from capital discipline and low valuations. Second, expectations of an increase in future defense spending across Europe lifted defense stocks, fueling strong returns for the industrials sector. While industrials are a significant cohort of the region’s quality growth stocks, defense stocks are more closely aligned with the value style.

Meanwhile, high-quality stocks have underperformed in part because many have expanded beyond Europe to accelerate growth, given their strong products and technology offerings. As a result, they tend to be more vulnerable to US tariffs and a weakening US dollar.

Now, however, we believe the stage is set for a recovery. Active investors can find select quality stocks trading at attractive discounts to cyclical growth stocks and at relatively low premiums to the broader market. To find them, you need to look beyond recent volatility and focus on long-term fundamentals.

Structural Growth Drivers Persist

Quality growth companies benefit from structural growth trends—such as digitization or automation—that are less dependent on the broader economic or market environment. When market trends turn against quality stocks, it doesn’t necessarily mean that the structural growth foundation upon which quality businesses are built has been impaired.

For example, in the specialty chemicals industry, manufacturers are increasingly outsourcing distribution to cut costs and improve efficiency. This creates an attractive growth backdrop for companies like IMCD (OTCPK:IMDZF)(OTCPK:IMCDY) of the Netherlands, a pure-play global specialty chemical distributor. IMCD’s exclusive long-term distribution agreements with many chemical producers position it well in a highly fragmented market. While the company’s end markets are currently depressed, its resilient business model, solid margins and growing market share create a strong foundation to thrive in a potential industry recovery.

Beijer Ref of Sweden offers another quality model that can defy market headwinds. The company, a wholesaler of heating, ventilation and air conditioning systems, benefits from increased demand as climate change reshapes heating and cooling needs. Since more than 80% of its business comes from repair and replacement orders, Beijer Ref’s quality business model provides more visibility into growth and less exposure to economic cycles, positioning the company to capitalize on long-term growth drivers.

Structural industry growth drivers are rarely derailed by economic weakness. When stock prices and valuations in these industries detach from their underlying growth trends, we see this as a temporary anomaly. During the COVID-19 pandemic, we saw the valuations of many quality companies get ahead of their fundamentals, while this year we believe the opposite is true. Over time, market dynamics typically normalize and realign with the positive industry environment, rewarding patient investors.

Resilient Business Models Can Endure

Quality companies typically exhibit durable business features such as high and consistent profitability, competitive advantages and strong balance sheets. Strong competitive edges and high entry barriers help companies sustain growth through changing market climates. Skilled management and disciplined capital allocation are key to navigating uncertainty. Solid financial metrics, like high returns on equity and invested capital, support steady cash flow and earnings growth.

Ryanair (OTCPK:RYAOF)(RYAAY) is a case in point. The low-cost airline is expanding market share via a quality business model, based on its growing cost advantage versus peers and significant net cash position. The company also owns all its aircraft outright, which provides additional financial stability and flexibility. Strong profitability has enabled the budget airline to build formidable competitive advantages, for example, investing countercyclically by purchasing 300 new aircraft at deep discounts during the COVID-19 pandemic and, more recently, by investing in its own engine repair shops.

Follow the Long Earnings Trail

Solid companies may suffer short-term lapses in stock performance that don’t necessarily signal a fundamental erosion of their long-term potential. That’s because earnings and cash flows are the best predictor of equity returns over long time horizons.

Of course, investors must always be alert to the possibility of a negative turn in a company’s earnings outlook that warrants reducing or selling a position. However, absent signs of fundamental deterioration, we think investors shouldn’t discard conviction in a long-term investment thesis because of unrelated wobbles in a share price.

Today’s market environment is also subject to heightened uncertainty on many fronts. Artificial intelligence exuberance, simmering trade tensions and macroeconomic fragility all suggest that market sentiment could quickly change. As we see it, a reassessment of risk could prompt a rotation toward quality stocks that have recently been out of favor.

Selective investors who target quality growth companies can find portfolio candidates trading at especially attractive valuations, based on a long-term outlook.

In fact, we think a five- to 10-year time horizon is the best way for equity investors to capitalize on opportunities. Just as business owners don’t change strategy because of a weak spell, equity investors with a strategic view of business health can develop conviction in earnings sustainability for many years ahead. With a view to the distant future, investors can resist succumbing to market pressures that often prove temporary and enjoy an added potential bonus of the compounding effect on returns when a recovery materializes.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

References to specific securities are presented to illustrate the application of our investment philosophy only and are not to be considered recommendations by AB. The specific securities identified and described do not represent all of the securities purchased, sold or recommended for the portfolio, and it should not be assumed that investments in the securities identified were or will be profitable.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Existing Home Sales Rise 1.2% To 8-Month High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Existing home sales rose 1.2% in October to their highest level in eight months. According to the National Association of Realtors (NAR), existing home sales were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.10 million units, exceeding the expected 4.08 million. Existing home sales are up 1.7% compared to one year ago.

"Home sales increased in October even with the government shutdown due to homebuyers taking advantage of lower mortgage rates," said NAR Chief Economist Lawrence Yun. "First-time homebuyers are facing headwinds in the Northeast due to a lack of supply and in the West because of high home prices. First-time buyers fared better in the Midwest because of the plentiful supply of affordable houses and in the South because there is sufficient inventory."

"Rents are decelerating, which will reduce inflation and encourage the Federal Reserve to continue cutting rates and pulling back their quantitative tightening," Yun added. "This will help bring more homebuyers into the market since the Fed rate has an indirect impact on mortgage rates." (Press release)

Background on Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales measures the monthly sales of previously owned single-family homes. It makes up a huge part of the residential real estate market, as 90% of purchased homes are previously owned, according to the National Association of Realtors. More importantly, there exists a strong correlation between purchases of existing homes and consumer spending. An increase in existing home sales can indirectly stimulate economic activity with increased consumer spending on new furnishings and appliances. Alternatively, a sustained drop in existing home sales often foreshadows a downturn in the economy.

Here is a snapshot of the data series, which comes from the National Association of Realtors. The data since January 1999 was previously available in the St. Louis Fed's FRED repository and is now only available for the last twelve months.

Over this time frame, we clearly see the real estate bubble, which peaked in 2005 and then fell dramatically. Sales were volatile for the first year or so following the Great Recession, with monthly sales as low as 3.45 million units to as high as 5.44 million units. We have seen that same volatility following the most recent recession, with sales ranging from 3.85 million units to 6.73 million units.

Existing Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Now let's examine the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show a 23.4% increase in the US population since the turn of the century. The snapshot below is an overlay of the NAR's annualized estimates with a population-adjusted version.

Existing home sales are 21.6% below the NAR's January 2000 estimate. The population-adjusted version is 35.7% below the turn-of-the-century sales.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed rate mortgage in October 2025 was 6.25%, the lowest level in over a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

Existing Home Sales: Median Price

The median price for existing homes rose for the first time in four months, coming in at $415,200. This represents a 0.7% increase from the previous month and a 2.1% increase from one year ago, marking the 28th consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: 2 IPOs Come To Market, Pipeline Grows Ahead Of Thanksgiving Holiday Week
data.description:
Two IPOs and one SPAC debuted this week. Four IPOs and seven SPACs submitted initial filings.

Missouri-based bank Central Bancompany (CBC) priced its Nasdaq uplisting at the low end of the range to raise $373 million at a $5.0 billion market cap. Central is a super-community bank with 156 full-service branches across 17 MSAs, with business predominantly located in Missouri, though it is growing its presence in the states of Kansas, Oklahoma, Colorado, and Florida. It finished the week up 12%.

Faith-focused technology platform Gloo Holdings (GLOO) priced its IPO below the range to raise $73 million at a $582 market cap. Gloo provides a vertical platform to faith-based organizations, with an emphasis on churches and Christian organizations, serving approximately 57,000 paying customers as of July 31, 2025. It finished the week up 19%.

One SPAC also priced this week. Hall Chadwick Acquisition (HCACU) raised $180 million to target tech, minerals, and energy.

3 IPOs During the Week of November 17th, 2025

Blank check company formed by Australian advisory firm Hall Chadwick targeting tech, minerals, and energy.

Four IPOs submitted initial filings this week. Satellite manufacturer York Space Systems (YSS) filed to raise an estimated $400 million. Diagnostic imaging provider Lumexa Imaging Holdings (LMRI) filed to raise an estimated $200 million. Collapsible shipping container developer GenFlat Holdings (OTCPK:GFLT) filed to raise $15 million at a $74 million market cap. Rare disease biotech Polaryx Therapeutics (PLYX) filed to complete a direct listing on the Nasdaq.

Seven SPACs also submitted initial filings. Michael Klein’s Churchill Capital XI (CCXIU) filed to raise $300 million. Infinite Eagle Acquisition (IEAGU), led by SPAC veteran Jeff Sagansky and former MGM CEO Harry Sloan, also filed to raise $300 million. D. Boral Acquisition (DBCAU), led by D. Boral Capital executives, filed to raise $250 million. Praetorian Acquisition (PTORU) filed to raise $220 million to target businesses that can be transformed by AI. Ithaca Capital Partners’ ITHAX Acquisition III (ITHAU) filed to raise $200 million. Breeze Acquisition II (BREZU) filed to raise $125 million to target global healthcare, tech, and robotics. Digital asset-focused Aperture AC (APURU) filed to raise $90 million.

Week Ahead

There is currently one small IPO currently scheduled in the week ahead, although some small deals may join the calendar at the last minute.

New IPO filings could come online, as the SEC continues to work through a backlog of applications received during the government shutdown. A number of names on file may also launch in the week ahead, as the window narrows to list this year. Among the names that could target December listings are medical supplies provider Medline (MDLN), pan-Asian travel platform Klook (KLK), and construction company Cardinal Infrastructure (CDNL).

Israeli regenerative medicine company Regentis Biomaterials (RGNT) is scheduled to raise $11 million at a $62 million market cap. Its lead candidate is GelrinC, a cell-free, off-the-shelf hydrogel implant for painful injuries to articular knee cartilage. GelrinC was approved as a device in Europe in 2017, and the company is currently carrying out a pivotal trial for pre-marketing approval in the US.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 11/20/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was down 2.2% year-to-date, while the S&P was up 12.4%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 31.7% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 26.6%. data.title: The Gold Miners Crushing Bullion
data.description:
As someone who’s been involved in capital markets his entire adult life, I can safely say that gold investors haven’t seen a period like this in decades. The third quarter of 2025 was nothing short of historic, and in many ways, I believe we’re witnessing the beginning of a new era for the yellow metal.

According to the World Gold Council (WGC), global demand reached 1,313 metric tons in Q3, the highest quarterly figure ever recorded. In dollar terms, the world spent $146 billion on gold, another record, while the metal broke 13 new all-time highs during the quarter alone. Prices averaged $3,456 an ounce, up 40% from a year earlier.

And then came October. Gold blasted through $4,400 for the first time ever before cooling off. Even after a normal, expected pullback, the metal continues to trade above $4,100.

The Love Trade and Fear Trade Working in Tandem

The gold drivers in Q3 included all the usual suspects. Central banks continued to diversify away from the U.S. dollar, adding another 220 metric tons. This brought total buying in 2025 so far to 634 tons, far above what we saw in the decade before the pandemic, according to the WGC.

Investors also poured into gold ETFs at the fastest pace since 2020. Meanwhile, demand for bars and coins stayed above the 300-ton mark for the fourth straight quarter, something we haven’t seen in over 12 years.

If you need another reason, the Federal Reserve is expected to continue cutting rates, though a December rate cut is now being called into question. Long-term real yields are softening, historically a tailwind for gold. The Love Trade and Fear Trade are working in tandem.

Our Best-Performing Gold Mining Stocks

Against this backdrop, gold mining stocks have surged. As part of our investment process, we always examine which names are outperforming gold itself, both quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) and year-over-year (YoY). I wanted to share several of the names, which we hold in at least one of our metals and mining funds. All data is as of September 30 and priced in local currency, mostly the Canadian dollar.

Barrick Mining (LEAPS Calls)

+207% in Q3

Let’s start with the standout in the QoQ category. Barrick Mining (B), which we have exposure to through long-dated January 2027 call options, was up an impressive 207% in Q3.

Barrick, which is considering a move from Toronto to the U.S., has been a textbook turnaround story. After years of lagging peers, the company has sharpened its focus, sold off non-core assets, and made free cash flow growth and balance sheet strength top priorities.

When you combine a disciplined senior producer with gold above $4,000 an ounce, you get powerful margin expansion. And because we held Barrick through LEAP options rather than common shares, the torque to rising gold prices was magnified. The strategy paid off handsomely.

McFarlane Lake Mining

+341% in Q3

Another strong performer this year has been McFarlane Lake Mining (OTCQB:MLMLF), up 341% in Q3. McFarlane, also based in Toronto, focuses on the Juby Gold Project in Ontario’s Abitibi Greenstone Belt, a region that’s produced more than 200 million ounces of gold over the past century.

After acquiring Juby from Aris Mining in September, McFarlane updated its mineral estimate to roughly 1 million ounces; the surrounding land package, which includes the McMillan property, offers meaningful exploration potential.

McFarlane’s leadership team has decades of mine-building experience in Ontario, and they’ve earned credibility within the investment community by moving quickly on permitting and geological work. As gold prices have climbed, investors have rewarded junior explorers with scale and momentum, and McFarlane was one of the clearest beneficiaries.

Omai Gold Mines

+650% for 12-Month Period

Let’s turn to names that dominated on a YoY basis. The single biggest mover in our portfolio over the past 12 months was Omai Gold Mines (OTCQB:OMGGF), which climbed an extraordinary 650% through the end of September. The Toronto-based company operates the historic Omai Mine in Guyana, one of the most prolific gold regions in South America.

Investors love brownfield opportunities with proven geology and existing infrastructure, and Omai has delivered exactly that. In an August report, the company reported 2.1 million ounces Indicated and 4.4 million ounces Inferred across its Wenot open-pit system and the deeper, high-grade Gilt Creek, both in Guyana.

An earlier economic study of Wenot, released in April 2024, estimated a net present value (NPV) of more than half a billion dollars at $1,950 gold. At today’s (much) higher gold price, the project’s valuation has undoubtedly soared.

Others

Other names in the gold mining sector that also delivered exceptional performance in Q3 included Andean Precious Metals, Vault Minerals and Felix Gold.

Andean, up 183% in Q3, reported the best financial results in its history, with record revenue, record earnings, and a record cash position. Vault Minerals, up 59%, saw its own record quarter, with more than 92,000 ounces produced, $703 million in cash and bullion, and no debt. Australia-based Felix Gold, up an incredible 351% in the third quarter, continued to advance its gold-and-antimony discovery in Alaska’s Fairbanks district, positioning itself not just as a precious metal explorer but also as part of the U.S.’s emerging domestic supply chain for critical minerals.

Preparing for the Next Leg Higher

Looking ahead, I believe the setup for Q4 remains constructive. Gold has already had a phenomenal year, rising 66% year-to-date at its high on October 20, but the forces at work this cycle remain. Fiscal deficits are widening. Geopolitical tensions are rising. Central banks are diversifying away from the greenback.

At the same time, the supply side of the gold mining industry isn’t expanding fast enough to keep up with demand. Major discoveries are rare, and average mine lead times are long, averaging 15 years between discovery and production.

Some analysts, at JPMorgan and elsewhere, now see gold reaching $5,000 as soon as next year if real yields fall further and political risks increase. I believe we could see $7,000 gold by the end of President Trump’s second term as the amount of debt around the globe continues to accelerate.

As always, I recommend a 10% weighting in gold, with 5% in physical bullion and 5% in high-quality gold mining stocks, mutual funds, and/or ETFs. Remember to rebalance at least once a year.

data.title: Marfrig Global Foods Will Need Packaged Food Growth To Offset Commodity Pressures
data.description:
Cyclicality is a fact of life in commodity businesses, and Marfrig Global Foods (OTCPK:MBRFY) has ample experience with the ups and downs of the global protein cycles. With the U.S. beef business likely to be in a tough spot for a few years due to high costs and a slow rebuild of herds and the acquired BRF poultry business likely facing a cyclical peak, Marfrig will need to lean heavily on the growing packaged foods business that BRF brought with it in this year’s merger.

Valuation is a bit complicated at this point, as below-trend margins in the near term complicate multiples-based approaches, and long-term free cash flow modeling is challenging at best given the cyclicality of the global protein markets. I do think that Marfrig is undervalued, and potentially meaningfully so if the company’s cost synergy and packaged food efforts pan out, but the next 12-24 months are likely to see meaningful headwinds for the business.

Q3 Results Were Decent, Given The Circumstances

The fiscal third quarter marked the first for the new Marfrig, and all things considered, it was a respectable performance in a tough environment.

Revenue rose 9% year over year and 7% quarter over quarter, with double-digit growth in the North American (up 12% yoy) and South American (up 18% yoy) beef businesses and 5% growth in the BRF operations. Volume was down about 6% in North American beef, but offset by strong realized pricing (up 20%) as beef prices have shot up in response to supply challenges.

While the BRF business was hampered by restrictions on poultry imports from Brazil in China and the EU (due to bird flu), domestic demand was healthy on an improving economy, demand from markets like Saudi Arabia and Korea helped offset some of the import restrictions, and packaged food sales grew about 7% as the company continued to gain share.

Margins were less positive. Beef packing gross profits are well below their long-term average across the sector on higher cattle prices, and Marfrig saw gross margin in the North American business fall 90 bps to 3.6%. Overall gross margin fell 160 bps to 12.3%, with South American gross margin down 70 bps to 16.4% and BRF down 260 bps to 24.7%.

This fed through to the EBITDA line, with adjusted EBITDA down almost 9% yoy (margin down 160 bps to 8.4%). North American EBITDA was down 6% yoy (margin down 40 bps to 2%), and BRF EBITDA was down 15% (margin down 370 bps to 15.5%), while South American EBITDA was up almost 32% (with margin up 120 bps to 11.1%).

Debt is a meaningful consideration here, with the company ending the quarter with a 3.1x net debt-to-EBITDA ratio.

Beef Likely To Be Pressured For Some Time

The U.S. beef market is not in a great place right now, with volumes down on shrinking herds (the July U.S. cattle herd was the smallest in more than 50 years) and correspondingly lower slaughter levels. That has sent cattle prices much higher (up almost 27% recently), and put pressure on packers like Marfrig, JBS (JBS), and Tyson (TSN) as realized prices aren’t keeping pace.

To that end, the recent U.S. decision to roll back tariffs on Brazilian beef won’t be any real help to Marfrig or JBS.

Likewise, biology limits how quickly the herd can be rebuilt, and it’s likely going to take around two or three years for Marfrig’s business to get back to more normal EBITDA margins (around 6%).

The Poultry Cycle Could Be More Supportive Than Previously Thought

As I’ve written in the past in reference to BRF, the global chicken market is likely peaking, and that could pressure results in 2026 and beyond. Lower feed prices have been supporting producer margins, and poultry’s relative affordability has been helping demand (particularly in light of higher prices for other proteins), but countries like the Philippines, Thailand, Ukraine, and Vietnam have been increasing their export volumes at a pace that threatens prices and margins next year (not to mention growth in volumes from the U.S. and Brazil).

While the global poultry market may not be so strong next year, Marfrig’s particular experience could be a little better. For starters, the company enjoys a good share in Turkey and the Middle East, and further growth here (including a recent joint venture with the Saudi Arabian sovereign wealth fund called Sadia Halal) should be a positive for the business.

I also believe the Brazilian poultry market could be in better shape next year. A healthy economy (including recent record-low unemployment) has made poultry more affordable for more people, and capacity constraints for slaughtering and cold storage in Brazil are likely to limit slaughter volume growth to only 1% or 2% next year. While companies are investing in capacity, it takes time to build and come on line, so the next 12-18 months are looking pretty good for Marfrig.

Packaged/Processed Food Remains The Key Longer-Term Driver

In the long years that I followed BRF (and before that, Sadia), the company’s packaged/processed food business was always an important part of the story, and that is no less true now post the merger with Marfrig.

Processed foods are about 30% of the revenue base, and Marfrig enjoys a strong share in Brazil (over 41%), as well as markets like Turkey and the Middle East. Processed food margins can be double those of unprocessed proteins (or more), and growth here will help offset some of the near-term pressures on the beef business and drive higher overall margins over the long term. With the company investing meaningfully to further expand its processed food capacity, I do expect this to be a significant growth driver, and Marfrig may be able to leverage BRF’s brands into new markets over time.

The Outlook

Given the pressures on the beef and poultry businesses, I’m not expecting a lot of revenue growth over the next few years, but I do think global protein demand growth, expanded processed food volumes, and market share gains can drive respectable long-term revenue growth in the 4% to 5% range.

On the margin side, I do expect adverse beef and poultry cycles to create great margin pressures in 2026-27. Some of that can be offset by further growth in the processed food business, but it also puts more pressure on management to hit their R$1B cost synergy target for the Marfrig-BRF deal. While I do think double-digit EBITDA margins could be possible over the long term, I’m not modeling that at this point, and I’m expecting margins in the 7%-8% range for a couple of years.

Looking at free cash flow, I don’t expect the company to achieve 4%+ FCF margins on a sustained basis given the pressures from commodity cycles, but there will likely be a few years comfortably above (and below) that level. Long term, I’m expecting FCF margins to average out in the 3%’s, driving mid-single-digit FCF growth (around 6% or so).

Discounted cash flows suggest that Marfrig could be meaningfully undervalued and priced for over 10% long-term annualized total returns, but there’s significant uncertainty when modeling out a commodity business beyond a few years. On the flip side, the weaker near-term outlook for margins makes multiple-based approaches (EV/EBITDA, P/E, et al) challenging as well, since the near-term earnings aren’t really representative of the company’s earning power. In any case, a blended forward multiple of 5.75x (weighing in both the higher-multiple packaged food business and lower-multiple unprocessed protein businesses) on my 2028 EBITDA discounted back two years gets me a fair value of around $4/share.

The Bottom Line

With the shares up about a third from their mid-October lows, Marfrig shares are in a weird place. I think the outlook for U.S. beef and global poultry over the next 12 months is challenging, and odds are that this stock will have above-average volatility. I think the business mix is solid, though, and I can see a case for a higher valuation over time. Given a fair bit of negativity in sentiment already, I think this is a name that patient, value-oriented investors could consider here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cellebrite DI: Strong Q3, But Valuation And Technical Issues Could Weigh On The Stock (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This article is ongoing coverage of Cellebrite DI (CLBT), which I last wrote about in November 2024. In my last article, I discussed how Cellebrite had strong long-term growth potential. I also explained that the valuation increase and the technical set-up could lead to a profit-taking pullback. It looks like the higher valuation and the technical level led to a sell-off from February to July 2025. The stock has dropped 9% since my last article.

Cellebrite's Q3 2025 earnings results provide insights into the company's potential performance. I currently see a potential issue again with the valuation. The technical level also looks bearish, which could lead to continued selling in the stock.

Cellebrite's Q3 2025 Results

Cellebrite did beat analysts' estimates for the top and bottom lines in Q3 2025. Revenue increased 18% yoy to $126 million, beating estimates by $3 million. Earnings per share remained flat yoy at $0.14, but beat analysts' estimates by $0.01.

Cellebrite's total revenue increased 18% yoy to $126 million in Q3 2025. This was a result of a 21% gain in subscription revenue. Subscription-based software solutions comprised about 89% of total revenue.

CLBT's annual recurring revenue [ARR] increased 19% yoy to $440 million in Q3 2025. This gain was attributed to increased spending by Cellebrite's existing customers. Cellebrite also achieved a 5% sequential gain over Q2 2025 for ARR. Geographically, the Americas comprised 55% of ARR, the EMEA comprised 33%, and Asia Pacific comprised 12%.

The gross profit increased 15.6% to $106.5 million. However, the gross margin declined from 85.6% in Q3 2024 down to 84.9% in Q3 2025 due to a 31.5% YoY increase in the cost of revenue. CLBT pointed out in the Q3 conference call that these results reflected the ongoing investment in cloud infrastructure as they rolled out Guardian and invested in the federal ATO process.

The operating margin declined from 18.2% in Q3 2024 to 15% in Q3 2025. However, the net income margin increased from a negative (194%) up to a positive 16% over the same time. The difference in the net income margin was a result of a large one-time interest expense of $224 million in Q3 2024.

Adjusted EBITDA increased 20% yoy to $37.7 million, while the adjusted EBITDA margin increased to 29.9% over the 29% from Q3 2024. This growth was achieved through disciplined hiring activity and strategic capital allocation across the organization.

Cellebrite experienced more customers turning to its cloud and SaaS offerings to manage their digital forensics and investigative workflows. CLBT pointed out in the Q3 conference call that its Guardian solution is emerging as the industry standard for the storage and collaboration of sensitive evidential artifacts.

The number of Guardian customers more than doubled YoY as ARR increased by at least 100% for the 5th consecutive quarter. The company can add to this growth as it will launch Guardian Investigate (an AI-powered investigative software designed for investigative teams to resolve cases faster) in Q1 2026.

Cellebrite narrowed its guidance for full year revenue. The company expects revenue to be in the range of $470 million to $475 million for 2025. This is a narrower range than CLBT's previous range of $465 million to $475 million at the end of Q2 2025. The analysts' consensus estimate is $473 million. CLBT also increased its guidance for adjusted EBITDA to be $124 million to $127 million. This was higher than the range given at the end of Q2 which was $118 million to $123 million.

Cellebrite's Growth Drivers

Cellebrite has multiple factors that can drive long-term growth. The launch of Guardian Investigate that I previously mentioned is one of these drivers. The company is targeting a launch in Q1 2026. By speeding up the process to resolve cases, CLBT can help investigative teams to save time and money.

CLBT sees expansion opportunities ahead for Inseyets, the company's digital forensics software suite. Cellebrite expects to drive broader adoption for Inseyets by extending their reach to new user groups and by upselling advanced unlock solutions and automation offerings. The company also expects to win new logos and to increase price or mix on existing operators. CLBT expects these growth drivers to result in high single-digit percentage growth in 2026.

Cellebrite also expects its Guardian and Pathfinder solutions to act as growth drivers. The company is anticipating healthy demand for Guardian from its core markets. CLBT also expects to expand Guardian in the U.S. Federal sector, in Australia and other international markets, and from sales to enterprises. The company also expects to achieve new business expansion and a stronger renewal rate for Pathfinder. These two solutions are expected to contribute mid single-digit ARR percentage growth.

The Corellium acquisition is another future growth driver. Cellebrite announced its intention to acquire Corellium back in June 2025. Corellium is a leader in Arm-based virtualization software. This will add to Cellebrite's current offerings. CLBT plans to sell Corellium's solutions to its existing customer base in the defense, enterprise, and intelligence categories. The acquisition is expected to close during Q4 2025 according to Cellebrite. CLBT expects Corellium to increase the company's total addressable market [TAM] and total value proposition. Corellium is expected to add a couple of percentage points to CLBT's AAR growth rate, while costing the company $150 million to $160 million for the purchase.

Valuation

I have concerns since Cellebrite's valuation level increased over the past year. I used the PEG ratio since that takes multiple years of earnings growth into the equation. Plus, Cellebrite has been a strong growth company with double-digit revenue and earnings growth. CLBT's PEG ratio increased from 1.72 in November 2024 when I last wrote about the company, up to the current level of 2.08. This is higher than the sector median PEG of 1.57. I typically like to see the PEG ratio below two.

Adding to the overvaluation is the forward PE ratio of 34x, which is much higher than the sector median of 22x. The forward price/sales of 9x is also much higher than the sector median of 3. The stock may lag the broader market with its valuation at these high levels.

CLBT is expected to grow earnings at 21% in 2025, but follow that up with a lower growth rate of 7.4% in 2026 (consensus). The lower growth rate for 2026 is likely the result of paying for the Corellium acquisition. These earnings growth rates can be achieved with the expected revenue growth of about 18% for 2025 and about 17% for 2026. The company's net income margin expansion that I mentioned earlier can drive earnings to hit the expected target for 2025.

Technical Perspective

We can see that CLBT's stock gapped up after the Q3 earnings report. However, the stock has dropped from about $20 down to the current price of $16.81 since then. The RSI indicator turned bearish as it moved below the 50 level. The MACD is not yet bearish since the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line. The MACD histogram bars are declining in length, suggesting waning momentum. The MACD would officially turn bearish when the blue line crosses below the red signal line while the histogram bars turn back to red.

I zoomed out to Cellebrite's weekly chart above. This shows the stock failing to rise above $21 back in March and failing to rise above $20 earlier this November. We now have two lower highs in the stock price, which looks bearish. The RSI is also bearish on the weekly chart as it fell back below the 50 level. The MACD looks like it is about to turn bearish as the blue line appears close to crossing below the red signal line. The histogram bars have been declining in length, suggesting the waning momentum could cause the bars to turn red below the zero line if the bearish crossover occurs.

Given the high valuation and this technical weakness, I think the most likely scenario for the stock is continued selling over the next few weeks or months. At the very least, I expect the stock to lag the broader market in the near term due to the stock's overvaluation.

Additional Risks for the Stock

Competition remains an ongoing threat to Cellebrite. Competition is intense for these types of software solutions. Many competitors are larger than Cellebrite, which means they have more financial and other resources to expand, develop products, and make acquisitions. Consolidation in the industry could result in pricing pressures. Competition could make it difficult for Cellebrite to grow at a strong pace in the future.

Cellebrite depends on obtaining business from law enforcement and government agencies. CLBT derived 90% of revenue from law enforcement and government agencies in 2022, 2023, and 2024. Less spending from these customers could significantly harm Cellebrite revenue.

The company depends on the renewal of subscriptions of its offerings. Reductions in subscriptions could have a negative impact on revenue and earnings. The company may also struggle to add new subscribers to its client list.

The Outlook For Cellebrite

I do think that Cellebrite had a solid Q3 2025 earnings report. I like the company's strong expected double-digit revenue growth for 2025 and 2026. I also like the net margin gains that were achieved in Q3. However, I still have concerns that the valuation is too high, which may cause the stock to underperform until this condition changes for the better. The technical outlook also looks bearish. As a result, I am going to lower my rating for the stock to a 'HOLD'.
